speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Here once again we sit, once again crowned

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And looked upon, I hope, with cheerful eyes.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: This “once again,” but that your Highness pleased,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Was once superfluous. You were crowned before,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: And that high royalty was ne’er plucked off,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: The faiths of men ne’er stainèd with revolt;

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Fresh expectation troubled not the land

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: With any longed-for change or better state.

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Therefore, to be possessed with double pomp,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: To guard a title that was rich before,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: To gild refinèd gold, to paint the lily,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: To throw a perfume on the violet,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: To smooth the ice or add another hue

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Unto the rainbow, or with taper-light

speaker: SALISBURY
text: To seek the beauteous eye of heaven to garnish,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Is wasteful and ridiculous excess.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: But that your royal pleasure must be done,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: This act is as an ancient tale new told,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: And, in the last repeating, troublesome,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Being urgèd at a time unseasonable.

speaker: SALISBURY
text: In this the antique and well-noted face

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Of plain old form is much disfigurèd,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: And like a shifted wind unto a sail,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: It makes the course of thoughts to fetch about,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Startles and frights consideration,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Makes sound opinion sick and truth suspected

speaker: SALISBURY
text: For putting on so new a fashioned robe.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: When workmen strive to do better than well,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: They do confound their skill in covetousness,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: And oftentimes excusing of a fault

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Doth make the fault the worse by th’ excuse,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: As patches set upon a little breach

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Discredit more in hiding of the fault

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Than did the fault before it was so patched.

speaker: SALISBURY
text: To this effect, before you were new-crowned,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: We breathed our counsel; but it pleased your Highness

speaker: SALISBURY
text: To overbear it, and we are all well pleased,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Since all and every part of what we would

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Doth make a stand at what your Highness will.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Some reasons of this double coronation

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: I have possessed you with, and think them strong;

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And more, more strong, when lesser is my fear,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: I shall endue you with. Meantime, but ask

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: What you would have reformed that is not well,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And well shall you perceive how willingly

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: I will both hear and grant you your requests.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Then I, as one that am the tongue of these

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: To sound the purposes of all their hearts,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Both for myself and them, but chief of all

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Your safety, for the which myself and them

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Bend their best studies, heartily request

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Th’ enfranchisement of Arthur, whose restraint

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Doth move the murmuring lips of discontent

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: To break into this dangerous argument:

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: If what in rest you have in right you hold,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Why then your fears, which, as they say, attend

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: The steps of wrong, should move you to mew up

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Your tender kinsman and to choke his days

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: With barbarous ignorance and deny his youth

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: The rich advantage of good exercise.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: That the time’s enemies may not have this

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: To grace occasions, let it be our suit

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: That you have bid us ask, his liberty,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Which for our goods we do no further ask

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Than whereupon our weal, on you depending,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Counts it your weal he have his liberty.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Let it be so. I do commit his youth

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: To your direction.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Hubert, what news with you?

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: This is the man should do the bloody deed.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: He showed his warrant to a friend of mine.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: The image of a wicked heinous fault

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Lives in his eye. That close aspect of his

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Doth show the mood of a much troubled breast,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: And I do fearfully believe ’tis done

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: What we so feared he had a charge to do.

speaker: SALISBURY
text: The color of the King doth come and go

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Between his purpose and his conscience,

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Like heralds ’twixt two dreadful battles set.

speaker: SALISBURY
text: His passion is so ripe it needs must break.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: And when it breaks, I fear will issue thence

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: The foul corruption of a sweet child’s death.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: We cannot hold mortality’s strong hand.—

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Good lords, although my will to give is living,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: The suit which you demand is gone and dead.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: He tells us Arthur is deceased tonight.

speaker: SALISBURY
text: Indeed, we feared his sickness was past cure.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Indeed, we heard how near his death he was

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Before the child himself felt he was sick.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: This must be answered either here or hence.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Why do you bend such solemn brows on me?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Think you I bear the shears of destiny?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Have I commandment on the pulse of life?

speaker: SALISBURY
text: It is apparent foul play, and ’tis shame

speaker: SALISBURY
text: That greatness should so grossly offer it.

speaker: SALISBURY
text: So thrive it in your game, and so farewell.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Stay yet, Lord Salisbury. I’ll go with thee

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: And find th’ inheritance of this poor child,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: His little kingdom of a forcèd grave.

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: That blood which owed the breadth of all this isle,

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: Three foot of it doth hold. Bad world the while!

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: This must not be thus borne; this will break out

speaker: PEMBROKE
text: To all our sorrows, and ere long, I doubt.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: They burn in indignation. I repent.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: There is no sure foundation set on blood,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: No certain life achieved by others’ death.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: A fearful eye thou hast. Where is that blood

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: That I have seen inhabit in those cheeks?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: So foul a sky clears not without a storm.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Pour down thy weather: how goes all in France?

speaker: MESSENGER
text: From France to England. Never such a power

speaker: MESSENGER
text: For any foreign preparation

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Was levied in the body of a land.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: The copy of your speed is learned by them,

speaker: MESSENGER
text: For when you should be told they do prepare,

speaker: MESSENGER
text: The tidings comes that they are all arrived.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: O, where hath our intelligence been drunk?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Where hath it slept? Where is my mother’s care,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: That such an army could be drawn in France

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And she not hear of it?

speaker: MESSENGER
text: My liege, her ear

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Is stopped with dust. The first of April died

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Your noble mother. And as I hear, my lord,

speaker: MESSENGER
text: The Lady Constance in a frenzy died

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Three days before. But this from rumor’s tongue

speaker: MESSENGER
text: I idly heard. If true or false, I know not.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Withhold thy speed, dreadful occasion!

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: O, make a league with me till I have pleased

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: My discontented peers. What? Mother dead?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: How wildly then walks my estate in France!—

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Under whose conduct came those powers of France

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: That thou for truth giv’st out are landed here?

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Under the Dauphin.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Thou hast made me giddy

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: With these ill tidings.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Now, what says the world

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: To your proceedings? Do not seek to stuff

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: My head with more ill news, for it is full.

speaker: BASTARD
text: But if you be afeard to hear the worst,

speaker: BASTARD
text: Then let the worst, unheard, fall on your head.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Bear with me, cousin, for I was amazed

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Under the tide, but now I breathe again

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Aloft the flood and can give audience

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: To any tongue, speak it of what it will.

speaker: BASTARD
text: How I have sped among the clergymen

speaker: BASTARD
text: The sums I have collected shall express.

speaker: BASTARD
text: But as I traveled hither through the land,

speaker: BASTARD
text: I find the people strangely fantasied,

speaker: BASTARD
text: Possessed with rumors, full of idle dreams,

speaker: BASTARD
text: Not knowing what they fear, but full of fear.

speaker: BASTARD
text: And here’s a prophet that I brought with me

speaker: BASTARD
text: From forth the streets of Pomfret, whom I found

speaker: BASTARD
text: With many hundreds treading on his heels,

speaker: BASTARD
text: To whom he sung in rude harsh-sounding rhymes

speaker: BASTARD
text: That ere the next Ascension Day at noon,

speaker: BASTARD
text: Your Highness should deliver up your crown.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Thou idle dreamer, wherefore didst thou so?

speaker: PETER
text: Foreknowing that the truth will fall out so.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Hubert, away with him! Imprison him.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And on that day at noon, whereon he says

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: I shall yield up my crown, let him be hanged.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Deliver him to safety and return,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: For I must use thee.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: O my gentle cousin,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Hear’st thou the news abroad, who are arrived?

speaker: BASTARD
text: The French, my lord. Men’s mouths are full of it.

speaker: BASTARD
text: Besides, I met Lord Bigot and Lord Salisbury

speaker: BASTARD
text: With eyes as red as new-enkindled fire,

speaker: BASTARD
text: And others more, going to seek the grave

speaker: BASTARD
text: Of Arthur, whom they say is killed tonight

speaker: BASTARD
text: On your suggestion.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Gentle kinsman, go

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And thrust thyself into their companies.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: I have a way to win their loves again.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Bring them before me.

speaker: BASTARD
text: I will seek them out.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Nay, but make haste, the better foot before!

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: O, let me have no subject enemies

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: When adverse foreigners affright my towns

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: With dreadful pomp of stout invasion.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Be Mercury, set feathers to thy heels,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And fly like thought from them to me again.

speaker: BASTARD
text: The spirit of the time shall teach me speed.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Spoke like a sprightful noble gentleman.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text:
To
                 
                Messenger
                .
                 
              Go after him, for he perhaps shall need

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Some messenger betwixt me and the peers,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And be thou he.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: With all my heart, my liege.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: My mother dead!

speaker: HUBERT
text: My lord, they say five moons were seen tonight—

speaker: HUBERT
text: Four fixèd, and the fifth did whirl about

speaker: HUBERT
text: The other four in wondrous motion.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Five moons!

speaker: HUBERT
text: Old men and beldams in the streets

speaker: HUBERT
text: Do prophesy upon it dangerously.

speaker: HUBERT
text: Young Arthur’s death is common in their mouths,

speaker: HUBERT
text: And when they talk of him, they shake their heads

speaker: HUBERT
text: And whisper one another in the ear,

speaker: HUBERT
text: And he that speaks doth grip the hearer’s wrist,

speaker: HUBERT
text: Whilst he that hears makes fearful action

speaker: HUBERT
text: With wrinkled brows, with nods, with rolling eyes.

speaker: HUBERT
text: I saw a smith stand with his hammer, thus,

speaker: HUBERT
text: The whilst his iron did on the anvil cool,

speaker: HUBERT
text: With open mouth swallowing a tailor’s news,

speaker: HUBERT
text: Who with his shears and measure in his hand,

speaker: HUBERT
text: Standing on slippers which his nimble haste

speaker: HUBERT
text: Had falsely thrust upon contrary feet,

speaker: HUBERT
text: Told of a many thousand warlike French

speaker: HUBERT
text: That were embattlèd and ranked in Kent.

speaker: HUBERT
text: Another lean, unwashed artificer

speaker: HUBERT
text: Cuts off his tale and talks of Arthur’s death.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Why seek’st thou to possess me with these fears?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Why urgest thou so oft young Arthur’s death?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Thy hand hath murdered him. I had a mighty cause

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: To wish him dead, but thou hadst none to kill him.

speaker: HUBERT
text: No had, my lord! Why, did you not provoke me?

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: It is the curse of kings to be attended

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: By slaves that take their humors for a warrant

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: To break within the bloody house of life,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And on the winking of authority

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: To understand a law, to know the meaning

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Of dangerous majesty, when perchance it frowns

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: More upon humor than advised respect.

speaker: HUBERT
text: Here is your hand and seal for what I did.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: O, when the last accompt twixt heaven and Earth

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Is to be made, then shall this hand and seal

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Witness against us to damnation!

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: How oft the sight of means to do ill deeds

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Make deeds ill done! Hadst not thou been by,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: A fellow by the hand of nature marked,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Quoted, and signed to do a deed of shame,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: This murder had not come into my mind.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: But taking note of thy abhorred aspect,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Finding thee fit for bloody villainy,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Apt, liable to be employed in danger,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: I faintly broke with thee of Arthur’s death;

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And thou, to be endearèd to a king,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Made it no conscience to destroy a prince.

speaker: HUBERT
text: My lord—

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Hadst thou but shook thy head or made a pause

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: When I spake darkly what I purposèd,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Or turned an eye of doubt upon my face,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: As bid me tell my tale in express words,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Deep shame had struck me dumb, made me break off,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And those thy fears might have wrought fears in me.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: But thou didst understand me by my signs

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And didst in signs again parley with sin,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Yea, without stop didst let thy heart consent

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And consequently thy rude hand to act

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: The deed which both our tongues held vile to name.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Out of my sight, and never see me more.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: My nobles leave me, and my state is braved,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Even at my gates, with ranks of foreign powers.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Nay, in the body of this fleshly land,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: This kingdom, this confine of blood and breath,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Hostility and civil tumult reigns

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Between my conscience and my cousin’s death.

speaker: HUBERT
text: Arm you against your other enemies.

speaker: HUBERT
text: I’ll make a peace between your soul and you.

speaker: HUBERT
text: Young Arthur is alive. This hand of mine

speaker: HUBERT
text: Is yet a maiden and an innocent hand,

speaker: HUBERT
text: Not painted with the crimson spots of blood.

speaker: HUBERT
text: Within this bosom never entered yet

speaker: HUBERT
text: The dreadful motion of a murderous thought,

speaker: HUBERT
text: And you have slandered nature in my form,

speaker: HUBERT
text: Which, howsoever rude exteriorly,

speaker: HUBERT
text: Is yet the cover of a fairer mind

speaker: HUBERT
text: Than to be butcher of an innocent child.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Doth Arthur live? O, haste thee to the peers,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Throw this report on their incensèd rage,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And make them tame to their obedience.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Forgive the comment that my passion made

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Upon thy feature, for my rage was blind,

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: And foul imaginary eyes of blood

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: Presented thee more hideous than thou art.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: O, answer not, but to my closet bring

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: The angry lords with all expedient haste.

speaker:
KING
               
              JOHN
text: I conjure thee but slowly; run more fast.
